slides.heading:
Passwordless,
and why your
==TOTP isn't saving you.==
slides.notes: Open strong. Mention this entire deck is one JSON document rendered through 9 fixed layouts.
slides.subheading: A tour through WebAuthn after a week of study. This copy of the deck is rendered from JSON — there is no HTML anywhere in the data.

slides.body:
~~"Passkeys vs. passwords."~~

=="Phishable auth vs. phishing-resistant auth."==

Everything we use today — passwords, SMS OTP, TOTP, push, magic links — is phishable. WebAuthn is the first widely-deployed primitive that isn't.
slides.heading: Ask the ==right question.==

columns: Real-time AiTM proxies the real page.
User sees the real URL — it IS the real site.
slides.footnote: Recent victims: Microsoft 365 admins · Cloudflare · Twilio · Reddit · Cisco · …
slides.heading: Phishing in 2026.
slides.notes: Land the point: this is commodity tooling, not nation-state capability.

slides.footnote: Login (assertion) is the same flow — fewer fields, same actors, same shape.
slides.heading: Registration, in one flow.

steps.body:
Generates a random challenge
Sends registration options

Calls the WebAuthn API
Bundles the REAL origin into `clientDataJSON`

Generates a key pair in hardware
Locks the private key to this site

Signs the challenge with the new key
Returns public key + signature

Verifies the signature
Stores the public key for this user

points.body: The private key never leaves hardware — Secure Enclave, StrongBox, the SE chip in a Yubikey. A server breach reveals only public keys.
points.label: 1 · NO SHARED SECRET

points.body: The browser bakes the requesting site's URL into `clientDataJSON`.
points.label: 2 · ORIGIN BINDING

points.body: 32 random bytes, signed once. No replay, no captured-code window.
points.label: 3 · FRESH CHALLENGE

points.body: The fingerprint never transits anywhere. It just tells the secure element: **this user is here, you may sign.**
points.label: 4 · BIOMETRIC GATES THE KEY

slides.body: The origin lives inside `clientDataJSON`, so the origin lives inside the signature. A phishing site cannot produce a signature the real server will accept — not "very hard", **mathematically cannot**.

slides.footnote: Most implementations check "is this WebAuthn? yes/no" and stop. Reading these two bits is a ==free policy upgrade.==
slides.heading: Four credential states, one prohibited.
rows:
Saved on your phone
or in the cloud

Can be backed up,
but hasn't been yet

Only works on one device
or a physical key

slides.caption: (placeholder) a screen recording of a login being phished
slides.heading: Watch a TOTP login get captured.

slides.body:
That attack didn't need malware. It didn't need to break TLS or cryptography.

**It just needed the user to type their secret on a proxy of the real site.**

==That's the entire class of attack WebAuthn defeats — structurally, not by user vigilance.==

items.a: Multi-credential enrolment + provider sync (iCloud, GPM) + cross-device QR sign-in. Recovery is solved.
items.q: What if I lose my phone?

items.a: WebAuthn shipped in Chrome 67 (2018). 99% of users have support.
iPhone Secure Enclave and Pixel StrongBox: yes. Software password-manager passkeys: still phishing-resistant, just no hardware key-extraction resistance.

Account lockout at the provider is rare. Recover through your provider's recovery email + security settings. Mitigation: **hardware key** as secondary factor, or multi-credential enrolment. This is why ==defence in depth== matters.
items.q: What if Apple or Google locks me out of my account?

slides.footnote: This deck: 11 slides of JSON · 9 layouts · zero HTML in the data.
slides.heading:
Phishing-resistant authentication
is no longer a luxury.
==Our second factors have been caught.==
slides.notes: Close, then segue into the builder demo if there's time.
slides.subheading: The standard, the browsers, the providers, the hardware — all of it shipped in the last 3 years.
